title: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura
chief_complaint: I have bruises all over my legs and arms. I didn't hit anything. My gums bleed when I brush. I had a nosebleed yesterday that wouldn't stop for 30 minutes. I noticed these tiny red dots on my ankles. I had a cold 2 weeks ago.

physical_exam.Skin: Diffuse petechiae on lower extremities. Multiple ecchymoses at various stages on arms and legs. No purpura. No mucosal bleeding.
physical_exam.HEENT: Gingival oozing at gum line. No epistaxis currently.
physical_exam.Neurologic: Normal. No retinal hemorrhages on fundoscopy.

hidden_data.cbc: WBC 8,500, Hb 12.5, Platelets 12,000 (isolated severe thrombocytopenia). Normal WBC and Hb.
hidden_data.peripheral_smear: Large platelets (megathrombocytes) consistent with accelerated thrombopoiesis. No schistocytes (rules out TTP). No blasts.
hidden_data.coagulation: PT/PTT normal. Fibrinogen normal. No DIC.

correct_diagnosis: Primary Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) - Isolated Severe Thrombocytopenia
key_learning_points: ITP = autoimmune destruction of platelets + impaired platelet production. Diagnosis of exclusion: Isolated thrombocytopenia with otherwise normal CBC, smear, and exam
Primary ITP vs Secondary: Secondary causes include SLE, HIV, Hepatitis C, H. pylori, CLL, drugs (heparin, quinine, sulfonamides, valproate)
Clinical: Mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae, ecchymoses, epistaxis, menorrhagia. Life-threatening bleeding rare (ICH <1%). Platelets >20K: Usually asymptomatic

Treatment: First-line: Prednisone 1mg/kg/day or Dexamethasone 40mg PO daily ×4 days. IVIG 1g/kg ×2 days if rapid rise needed or steroids contraindicated. Rituximab for refractory. TPO-agonists (Eltrombopag, Romiplostim) for chronic ITP
Splenectomy: Reserved for chronic refractory ITP after failure of medical therapy (2-3 lines). Must vaccinate before (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib)

Target platelets >20-30K (not normal). Major bleeding requires >50K. Avoid IM injections, NSAIDs, antiplatelets. Platelet transfusion only for life-threatening hemorrhage (platelets will be rapidly destroyed)
